record_office_terminology.land_records: Register of Deeds (county-level deed and land records)
record_office_terminology.probate: County Clerk (wills, estates); Chancery Court for contested estates
record_office_terminology.marriage_licenses: County Clerk (county-level from formation; statewide registration 1861)
record_office_terminology.vital_records_state_start: 1914 (births statewide registration; deaths from 1908; compliance improved by 1920s)

burned_county_authority: Tennessee State Library & Archives (TSLA), Guide to County Records; Byron Sistler & Associates, 'Guide to Tennessee County Records'; FamilySearch Research Wiki — Tennessee Burned Counties
administrative_notes: Tennessee was the 16th state (1796), formed from the Southwest Territory (1790-1796) and earlier Western Territory. Previously part of North Carolina — pre-1796 records may be in NC State Archives. The state has 95 counties in three Grand Divisions: East Tennessee (mountainous, Union-sympathizing in Civil War), Middle Tennessee (Nashville basin, agricultural), and West Tennessee (Mississippi River, cotton/plantation economy, opened to settlement after Jackson Purchase 1818). Two counties (James and Tennessee) existed briefly and were abolished before statehood. Tennessee State Library & Archives (TSLA) in Nashville is the primary repository, holding microfilmed county records for virtually all 95 counties.

core_repositories.description: Primary state repository. Houses microfilmed county records (deeds, marriages, wills, court minutes, tax lists), state government records, military records, vital records, and Confederate pension files. Extensive microfilm collection covers virtually all 95 counties. TSLA also holds Tennessee historical newspapers, maps, and photographs.
Digitized records from TSLA including photographs, manuscripts, maps, Civil War records, and selected county records. Provides online access to materials that previously required in-person visits.

Major East Tennessee genealogical and historical collection. Extensive manuscript collections, published genealogies, county histories, and microfilm. Particularly strong for East Tennessee counties. Calvin M. McClung Digital Collection provides online access to some materials.
Major West Tennessee genealogical collection. Holds Memphis city directories, Shelby County records, and resources for West Tennessee cotton plantation research including enslaved persons records.

familysearch_collections.description: Indexed and browsable collections: county records (deeds, marriages, wills, court records), vital records, cemetery records, military records, and church records. Extensive microfilm partnership with TSLA provides digitized county records for most Tennessee counties.
Comprehensive research guide with county-specific pages, record availability, burned county information, and links to digitized microfilm.

newspapers_and_periodicals.description: Digitized historic Tennessee newspapers from the 1790s forward. Includes major papers like the Nashville Banner, Knoxville Journal, Memphis Commercial Appeal, and county weeklies.
Digitized Tennessee newspapers not available in Chronicling America. County weeklies documenting legal notices, estates, and community events across rural Tennessee.

enslaved_persons_and_african_american.description: Guide to African American records at TSLA including: county tax lists naming enslaved by slaveholder, estate inventories, bills of sale, Colored Conventions records, and antebellum free person of color records. West Tennessee counties (Shelby, Fayette, Haywood, Tipton, Lauderdale) have the most extensive enslaved records due to cotton plantation economy.

Records of the Bureau of Refugees, Freedmen, and Abandoned Lands for Tennessee (1865-1872). Labor contracts, marriage registrations, school reports, and complaint files. Nashville was a major Freedmen's Bureau office. Many records indexed on FamilySearch.
Historically Black university with manuscript collections including Jubilee Singers records, Charles Spurgeon Johnson papers, and local African American community records for Middle Tennessee.

native_american_and_removal_records.description: Cherokee census rolls, property valuations, and emigration records. Red Clay (Bradley County) was the last capital of the Cherokee Nation East before removal (1838). Overhill Cherokee towns in Cocke, Sevier, Blount, and Monroe counties. Ross's Landing (Chattanooga/Hamilton County) was a major removal departure point. NARA Record Group 75.

Jackson Purchase (1818) opened West Tennessee for settlement. Chickasaw land cession records, early settler claims, and land entry records at TSLA and NARA. Covers all counties west of the Tennessee River (Shelby, Madison, Carroll, Gibson, etc.).

military_records.description: Confederate pension applications (1891-1921), Union Army of Tennessee records, War of 1812 records, Creek War records, and Mexican War records. Tennessee contributed soldiers to both sides in the Civil War — check both Confederate and Union records. WWII service cards. Pension files include family information and residence history.

military_records.source_name: Civil War Soldiers & Sailors System — Tennessee
military_records.description: Database of Confederate and Union soldiers from Tennessee. Tennessee was deeply divided — East Tennessee was strongly Unionist while Middle and West Tennessee had significant Confederate support. Major battles: Shiloh (Hardin), Stones River (Rutherford), Franklin (Williamson), Nashville (Davidson), Chattanooga (Hamilton).

land_and_property.description: Tennessee land grants from multiple sources: North Carolina (pre-statehood grants in current TN), federal land entries, and state grants. NC military bounty land warrants for Revolutionary War service were used extensively in Middle and West Tennessee. Tennessee did not use the federal public domain land system — records are at TSLA, not BLM/GLO.

vital_records.source_name: Tennessee Department of Health — Vital Records
vital_records.description: State vital records: deaths from 1908, births from 1914. TSLA has free online access to death records 1908-1964 and birth records 1908-1912. For earlier vital events check county records, church registers, and newspaper notices. Nashville, Memphis, Knoxville, and Chattanooga had earlier city registrations.

church_and_cemetery.description: Microfilmed church records from denominations across Tennessee. Baptist (most prevalent), Methodist, Presbyterian, Church of Christ (Stone-Campbell Movement originated in TN), and Cumberland Presbyterian (founded in Dickson County 1810) records are most common. Quaker communities in East Tennessee (Greene, Washington counties).
Crowdsourced cemetery transcriptions for Tennessee. Coverage varies — Appalachian family cemeteries may be under-documented. Mountain cemeteries are important for East Tennessee genealogy.

research_guides_and_societies.description: Statewide genealogical society based in Memphis. Publishes Ansearchin' News quarterly journal. Maintains research resources and county-level guides.
Regional historical society covering East Tennessee counties. Publishes Journal of East Tennessee History. Calvin McClung historical manuscript collections.
Volunteer-contributed county-level genealogy resources including cemetery transcriptions, Bible records, county histories, and research guides for all 95 Tennessee counties.

historical_counties_extinct.description: Tennessee has two briefly-existing extinct counties: James County (created 1871 from Bradley and Hamilton, abolished 1919 — merged back into Hamilton County) and Tennessee County (existed briefly in the 1780s in the western territory). Additionally, researchers should note that pre-1796 Tennessee was part of North Carolina — early land grants, court records, and governmental records may be found in NC State Archives (Raleigh). The Southwest Territory (1790-1796) had its capital at Knoxville. Washington District courts (pre-1790) generated records now held at TSLA and in Washington County, TN archives.

burned_counties_summary.notes: Tennessee courthouse record losses stem from Civil War destruction (especially in East Tennessee where Union and Confederate forces fought over territory), antebellum fires, and post-war courthouse fires. East Tennessee saw the most Civil War-related destruction due to its divided loyalties. TSLA microfilm often preserves copies of records from counties that experienced losses. Federal census records (at FamilySearch/Ancestry) are critical substitute sources for burned counties. The Sistler & Associates research guides document surviving records county by county.
